message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   Add EN_PERF benchmarking support to all 59 test files under `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/test/` that do NOT already have it. The already-done files to skip are: test_sin.c, test_cos.c, test_cosf.c.

   The four-step transformation for each file:
   1. After the `#include` block, add:
      ```c
      #ifdef EN_PERF
      #define PF_COUNTER
      #include "nds_pfcounter.h"
      #endif
      ```
   2. Keep d2u/u2d or f2u/u2f helpers OUTSIDE the `#ifndef EN_PERF` guard (they are needed by perf()). Wrap emit(), emit_u(), and ALL test_*() functions in `#ifndef EN_PERF ... #endif`.
   3. Add a `perf()` function inside `#ifdef EN_PERF ... #endif /* EN_PERF */` immediately after the closing `#endif` of the accuracy section. No critical-path comment block (unlike sin/cos templates).
   4. Update `main()` to branch: `#ifdef EN_PERF` / `perf(<input>)` / `#else` / all test calls / `#endif`.

   The complete list of 59 files:
   test_sinf.c, test_tan.c, test_tanf.c, test_acos.c, test_acosf.c, test_asin.c, test_asinf.c, test_atan.c, test_atanf.c, test_exp.c, test_expf.c, test_log.c, test_logf.c, test_log10.c, test_log10f.c, test_pow.c, test_powf.c, test_sqrt.c, test_sqrtf.c, test_sinh.c, test_sinhf.c, test_cosh.c, test_coshf.c, test_tanh.c, test_tanhf.c, test_ceil.c, test_ceilf.c, test_floor.c, test_floorf.c, test_fabs.c, test_fabsf.c, test_fmod.c, test_fmodf.c, test_frexp.c, test_frexpf.c, test_ldexp.c, test_ldexpf.c, test_modf.c, test_modff.c, test_fpclassify.c, test_fpclassifyf.c, test_nextafter.c, test_remainder.c, test_remquo.c, test_hypot.c, test_log1p.c, test_expm1.c, test_acosh.c, test_atanh.c, test_erf.c, test_erfc.c, test_lgamma.c, test_ilogb.c, test_lrint.c, test_lround.c, test_rint.c, test_round.c, test_trunc.c, test_fmax.c, test_fmin.c

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - EN_PERF macro: guards benchmark code vs. accuracy-test code in same source file
   - `nds_pfcounter.h`: Andes FPGA performance counter header; `startPFM`, `stopPFM`, `readResult()` macros
   - Double precision pattern: type=double, conv=d2u/u2d, cast=(unsigned long long), width=16, fmt=llx
   - Float precision pattern: type=float, conv=f2u/u2f, cast=(unsigned long), width=8, fmt=lx
   - Special-case perf() bodies for two-arg functions (pow, fmod, hypot, fmax, fmin, nextafter, remainder, remquo, powf, fmodf): fixed second arg, loop varies first arg via x+i
   - Output-parameter functions (frexp, modf, ldexp and float variants): declare local out-param variable inside perf()
   - Integer-returning functions (fpclassify, fpclassifyf, ilogb): volatile int acc, print as hex
   - Long-returning functions (lrint, lround): volatile long acc
   - Representative inputs per CLAUDE.md: trig=1.0/1.0f, exp=1.0/1.0f, log/log10=2.0/2.0f, log1p=1.0, pow/sqrt=2.0/2.0f, inverse trig=0.5/0.5f, hyperbolic=1.0/1.0f, ceil/floor/fabs/rint/round/trunc=1.5/1.5f, fmod=10.0/10.0f, frexp/ldexp=1.0/1.0f, modf=2.5/2.5f, nextafter/fmax/fmin=1.0/1.0f, remainder/remquo=10.0, hypot=3.0, acosh=2.0, atanh=0.5, erf/erfc=1.0, lgamma=1.5, ilogb/lrint/lround=1.5/1.5f

3. Files and Code Sections:

   Template files (reference, already had EN_PERF):
   - `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/test/test_sin.c` - double-precision EN_PERF template
   - `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/test/test_cosf.c` - float-precision EN_PERF template

   Files COMPLETED (written with EN_PERF support) in this session:
   - test_sinf.c - float, sinf, perf(1.0f)
   - test_tan.c - double, tan, perf(1.0)
   - test_tanf.c - float, tanf, perf(1.0f)
   - test_acos.c - double, acos, perf(0.5)
   - test_acosf.c - float, acosf, perf(0.5f)
   - test_asin.c - double, asin, perf(0.5)
   - test_asinf.c - float, asinf, perf(0.5f)
   - test_atan.c - double, atan, perf(0.5)
   - test_atanf.c - float, atanf, perf(0.5f)
   - test_exp.c - double, exp, perf(1.0)
   - test_expf.c - float, expf, perf(1.0f)
   - test_log.c - double, log, perf(2.0)
   - test_logf.c - float, logf, perf(2.0f)
   - test_log10.c - double, log10, perf(2.0)
   - test_log10f.c - float, log10f, perf(2.0f)
   - test_pow.c - double two-arg, pow(x+i, 0.5), perf(2.0)
   - test_powf.c - float two-arg, powf(x+i, 0.5f), perf(2.0f)
   - test_sqrt.c - double, sqrt, perf(2.0)
   - test_sqrtf.c - float, sqrtf, perf(2.0f)
   - test_sinh.c - double, sinh, perf(1.0); test funcs include test_critical
   - test_sinhf.c - float, sinhf, perf(1.0f); test funcs include test_threshold
   - test_cosh.c - double, cosh, perf(1.0); test funcs include test_critical
   - test_coshf.c - float, coshf, perf(1.0f); test funcs include test_threshold
   - test_tanh.c - double, tanh, perf(1.0); test funcs include test_critical
   - test_tanhf.c - float, tanhf, perf(1.0f); test funcs include test_saturation
   - test_ceil.c - double, ceil, perf(1.5); test funcs include test_boundary

   Files READ but NOT YET WRITTEN (last batch read before summary request):
   - test_ceilf.c - float, ceilf, perf(1.5f); uses test_boundaries (note: plural, different from test_boundary)
   - test_floor.c - double, floor, perf(1.5); uses test_boundary
   - test_floorf.c - float, floorf, perf(1.5f); uses test_boundaries (plural)

   Standard perf() template (double):
   ```c
   #ifdef EN_PERF
   static void perf(double x)
   {
       volatile double acc = 0.0;
       startPFM;
       for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)
           acc += <func>(x+i);
       stopPFM;
       readResult();
       printf("perf %016llx: %016llx\n",
              (unsigned long long)d2u(x),
              (unsigned long long)d2u((double)acc));
   }
   #endif /* EN_PERF */
   ```

   Standard perf() template (float):
   ```c
   #ifdef EN_PERF
   static void perf(float x)
   {
       volatile float acc = 0.0f;
       startPFM;
       for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)
           acc += <func>f(x+i);
       stopPFM;
       readResult();
       printf("perf %08lx: %08lx\n",
              (unsigned long)f2u(x),
              (unsigned long)f2u((float)acc));
   }
   #endif /* EN_PERF */
   ```

   Two-arg perf() example (pow double):
   ```c
   static void perf(double x)
   {
       volatile double acc = 0.0;
       startPFM;
       for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)
           acc += pow(x+i, 0.5);
       stopPFM;
       ...
   }
   ```

   Files read in prior session but not yet written (special-signature or remaining):
   - test_fabs.c, test_fabsf.c
   - test_fmod.c, test_fmodf.c
   - test_frexp.c, test_frexpf.c
   - test_ldexp.c, test_ldexpf.c
   - test_modf.c, test_modff.c
   - test_fpclassify.c, test_fpclassifyf.c
   - test_nextafter.c
   - test_remainder.c
   - test_remquo.c
   - test_hypot.c
   - test_log1p.c, test_expm1.c
   - test_acosh.c, test_atanh.c
   - test_erf.c, test_erfc.c
   - test_lgamma.c
   - test_ilogb.c
   - test_lrint.c, test_lround.c
   - test_rint.c, test_round.c, test_trunc.c
   - test_fmax.c, test_fmin.c

4. Errors and fixes:
   - None encountered. All file reads and writes completed successfully.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Systematic batch processing: reading 3 files at a time, then writing them in parallel
   - Special function categories identified and handled:
     - Two-argument functions: fixed second arg in loop (pow, fmod, hypot, fmax, fmin, nextafter, remainder, remquo)
     - Output-parameter functions (frexp, modf, ldexp): declare local variable inside perf()
     - Non-standard test function names preserved verbatim (test_boundaries vs test_boundary, test_critical vs test_saturation vs test_threshold, test_exponent_range, test_special_base, test_negative, etc.)

6. All user messages:
   - Initial task (prior session): "Add EN_PERF benchmarking support to all test files under `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/test/` that do NOT already have it." with full detailed instructions.
   - Current message: "Your task is to create a detailed summary of the conversation so far..."

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Write test_ceilf.c (read, not yet written)
   - Write test_floor.c (read, not yet written)
   - Write test_floorf.c (read, not yet written)
   - Read and write all remaining 33 files: test_fabs.c, test_fabsf.c, test_fmod.c, test_fmodf.c, test_frexp.c, test_frexpf.c, test_ldexp.c, test_ldexpf.c, test_modf.c, test_modff.c, test_fpclassify.c, test_fpclassifyf.c, test_nextafter.c, test_remainder.c, test_remquo.c, test_hypot.c, test_log1p.c, test_expm1.c, test_acosh.c, test_atanh.c, test_erf.c, test_erfc.c, test_lgamma.c, test_ilogb.c, test_lrint.c, test_lround.c, test_rint.c, test_round.c, test_trunc.c, test_fmax.c, test_fmin.c

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before the summary request, I had just read (but not yet written) three files:
   - test_ceilf.c: float version of ceil, uses f2u/u2f, ceilf, test_boundaries (note plural), perf input 1.5f
   - test_floor.c: double version of floor, uses d2u/u2d, floor, test_boundary (singular), perf input 1.5
   - test_floorf.c: float version of floor, uses f2u/u2f, floorf, test_boundaries (plural), perf input 1.5f

   All three have the same test structure (test_special, test_tiny, test_known, test_large, test_boundaries/test_boundary, test_hard, test_sweep, test_exhaustive_small, test_random_300) and need the standard EN_PERF transformation.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Write the three files that were read immediately before the summary request, then continue with the remaining files. Specifically:

   Write test_ceilf.c with EN_PERF support:
   - Insert EN_PERF header guard after includes
   - Move emit/emit_u/test_* inside `#ifndef EN_PERF`
   - Add float perf() with `ceilf(x+i)` and `perf(1.5f)` in main
   - test functions order: test_special, test_tiny, test_known, test_large, test_boundaries, test_hard, test_sweep, test_exhaustive_small, test_random_300

   Then write test_floor.c (double, floor, test_boundary singular, perf(1.5)) and test_floorf.c (float, floorf, test_boundaries plural, perf(1.5f)), then proceed through the remaining 31 files.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /home/nick/.claude/projects/-home-nick-work-picolibc-agentic-libm-machine-riscv/408e342a-8511-4f6d-aeb8-691d01eb229c.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

message.content:
Your task is to create a detailed summary of the conversation so far, paying close attention to the user's explicit requests and your previous actions.
This summary should be thorough in capturing technical details, code patterns, and architectural decisions that would be essential for continuing development work without losing context.

Before providing your final summary, wrap your analysis in <analysis> tags to organize your thoughts and ensure you've covered all necessary points. In your analysis process:

1. Chronologically analyze each message and section of the conversation. For each section thoroughly identify:
   - The user's explicit requests and intents
   - Your approach to addressing the user's requests
   - Key decisions, technical concepts and code patterns
   - Specific details like:
     - file names
     - full code snippets
     - function signatures
     - file edits
   - Errors that you ran into and how you fixed them
   - Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
2. Double-check for technical accuracy and completeness, addressing each required element thoroughly.

Your summary should include the following sections:

1. Primary Request and Intent: Capture all of the user's explicit requests and intents in detail
2. Key Technical Concepts: List all important technical concepts, technologies, and frameworks discussed.
3. Files and Code Sections: Enumerate specific files and code sections examined, modified, or created. Pay special attention to the most recent messages and include full code snippets where applicable and include a summary of why this file read or edit is important.
4. Errors and fixes: List all errors that you ran into, and how you fixed them. Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
5. Problem Solving: Document problems solved and any ongoing troubleshooting efforts.
6. All user messages: List ALL user messages that are not tool results. These are critical for understanding the users' feedback and changing intent.
7. Pending Tasks: Outline any pending tasks that you have explicitly been asked to work on.
8. Current Work: Describe in detail precisely what was being worked on immediately before this summary request, paying special attention to the most recent messages from both user and assistant. Include file names and code snippets where applicable.
9. Optional Next Step: List the next step that you will take that is related to the most recent work you were doing. IMPORTANT: ensure that this step is DIRECTLY in line with the user's most recent explicit requests, and the task you were working on immediately before this summary request. If your last task was concluded, then only list next steps if they are explicitly in line with the users request. Do not start on tangential requests or really old requests that were already completed without confirming with the user first.
                       If there is a next step, include direct quotes from the most recent conversation showing exactly what task you were working on and where you left off. This should be verbatim to ensure there's no drift in task interpretation.

Here's an example of how your output should be structured:

<example>
<analysis>
[Your thought process, ensuring all points are covered thoroughly and accurately]
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   [Detailed description]

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - [Concept 1]
   - [Concept 2]
   - [...]

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - [File Name 1]
      - [Summary of why this file is important]
      - [Summary of the changes made to this file, if any]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [File Name 2]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [...]

4. Errors and fixes:
    - [Detailed description of error 1]:
      - [How you fixed the error]
      - [User feedback on the error if any]
    - [...]

5. Problem Solving:
   [Description of solved problems and ongoing troubleshooting]

6. All user messages: 
    - [Detailed non tool use user message]
    - [...]

7. Pending Tasks:
   - [Task 1]
   - [Task 2]
   - [...]

8. Current Work:
   [Precise description of current work]

9. Optional Next Step:
   [Optional Next step to take]

</summary>
</example>

Please provide your summary based on the conversation so far, following this structure and ensuring precision and thoroughness in your response. 

There may be additional summarization instructions provided in the included context. If so, remember to follow these instructions when creating the above summary. Examples of instructions include:
<example>
## Compact Instructions
When summarizing the conversation focus on typescript code changes and also remember the mistakes you made and how you fixed them.
</example>

<example>

When you are using compact - please focus on test output and code changes. Include file reads verbatim.
</example>


IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any tools. You MUST respond with ONLY the <summary>...</summary> block as your text output.

content.text:
<analysis>
Let me analyze this conversation thoroughly.

The conversation is a continuation from a previous session (indicated by the system-provided summary). The task is to add EN_PERF benchmarking support to test files in `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/test/`.

From the summary, the previous session had:
- Already completed: test_sinf.c through test_tanhf.c (26 files)
- Read but not written: test_ceilf.c, test_floor.c, test_floorf.c

In this session (current), I:
1. Read test_ceilf.c, test_floor.c, test_floorf.c and wrote them
2. Read test_fabs.c, test_fabsf.c, test_fmod.c, test_fmodf.c, test_frexp.c, test_frexpf.c and wrote them
3. Read test_ldexp.c, test_ldexpf.c, test_modf.c, test_modff.c, test_fpclassify.c, test_fpclassifyf.c and wrote them
4. Read test_nextafter.c, test_remainder.c, test_remquo.c, test_hypot.c, test_log1p.c, test_expm1.c and wrote them
5. Read test_acosh.c, test_atanh.c, test_erf.c, test_erfc.c, test_lgamma.c, test_ilogb.c and wrote them
6. Read test_lrint.c, test_lround.c, test_rint.c (but NOT yet written them)

Still pending (read but not written in this session): test_lrint.c, test_lround.c, test_rint.c
Still not read or written: test_round.c, test_trunc.c, test_fmax.c, test_fmin.c

The transformation pattern:
1. Add EN_PERF header guard after includes
2. Keep d2u/u2d or f2u/u2f helpers OUTSIDE `#ifndef EN_PERF`
3. Wrap emit(), emit_u(), and all test_*() in `#ifndef EN_PERF ... #endif`
4. Add perf() inside `#ifdef EN_PERF ... #endif /* EN_PERF */`
5. Update main() to branch on EN_PERF

Special cases identified:
- lrint/lround: returns long, volatile long acc, print as %016lx
- rint: standard double, returns double
- round: standard double
- trunc: standard double
- fmax/fmin: two-arg functions
- fpclassify: returns int, volatile int acc
- ilogb: returns int, volatile int acc

The user's last message was asking for a summary (this message).

The current work was reading test_lrint.c, test_lround.c, and test_rint.c immediately before the summary request - those three were read but not yet written.

Next step would be to write those three files, then continue with test_round.c, test_trunc.c, test_fmax.c, test_fmin.c.
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   Add EN_PERF benchmarking support to all 59 test files under `/home/nick/work/picolibc_agentic/libm/machine/riscv/test/` that do NOT already have it. The already-done files to skip are: test_sin.c, test_cos.c, test_cosf.c.

   The four-step transformation for each file:
   1. After the `#include` block, add:
      ```c
      #ifdef EN_PERF
      #define PF_COUNTER
      #include "nds_pfcounter.h"
      #endif
      ```
   2. Keep d2u/u2d or f2u/u2f helpers OUTSIDE the `#ifndef EN_PERF` guard (they are needed by perf()). Wrap emit(), emit_u(), and ALL test_*() functions in `#ifndef EN_PERF ... #endif`.
   3. Add a `perf()` function inside `#ifdef EN_PERF ... #endif /* EN_PERF */` immediately after the closing `#endif` of the accuracy section. No critical-path comment block.
   4. Update `main()` to branch: `#ifdef EN_PERF` / `perf(<input>)` / `#else` / all test calls / `#endif`.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - EN_PERF macro: guards benchmark code vs. accuracy-test code in same source file
   - `nds_pfcounter.h`: Andes FPGA performance counter header; `startPFM`, `stopPFM`, `readResult()` macros
   - Double precision pattern: type=double, conv=d2u/u2d, cast=(unsigned long long), width=16, fmt=llx
   - Float precision pattern: type=float, conv=f2u/u2f, cast=(unsigned long), width=8, fmt=lx
   - Two-arg perf() bodies for fmod, hypot, fmax, fmin, nextafter, remainder, remquo: fixed second arg, loop varies first arg via x+i
   - Output-parameter functions (frexp, modf, ldexp and float variants): declare local out-param variable inside perf()

   Standard double perf() template used throughout:
   ```c
   #ifdef EN_PERF
   static void perf(double x)
   {
       volatile double acc = 0.0;
       startPFM;
       for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)
           acc += <func>(x+i);
       stopPFM;
       readResult();
       printf("perf %016llx: %016llx\n",
              (unsigned long long)d2u(x),
              (unsigned long long)d2u((double)acc));
   }
   #endif /* EN_PERF */
   ```

   Standard float perf() template:
   ```c
   #ifdef EN_PERF
   static void perf(float x)
   {
       volatile float acc = 0.0f;
       startPFM;
       for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)
           acc += <func>f(x+i);
       stopPFM;
       readResult();
       printf("perf %08lx: %08lx\n",
              (unsigned long)f2u(x),
              (unsigned long)f2u((float)acc));
   }
   #endif /* EN_PERF */
   ```

5. Problem Solving:
   - Systematic batch processing: reading 3-6 files at a time, then writing them in parallel
   - Special function categories handled:
     - Two-argument functions: fixed second arg in loop body (fmod, hypot, nextafter, remainder, remquo)
     - Output-parameter functions (frexp, modf, ldexp and float variants): declare local out-param variable before startPFM inside perf()
     - Integer-returning functions (fpclassify/f, ilogb): volatile int acc, print with %x
     - Long-returning functions (lrint, lround): need volatile long acc and %016lx format
     - atanh domain constraint: can't simply use x+i since values must be in (-1,1); used `atanh(x / (x+i+1.0))` to keep argument in domain
